sentences.text: Yeah, no.
I've got a lot of friends where they was in concentration camp.
I wasn't there in, in Zawichost.
But the teacher, Sabatowski, they told me.
They took-- they took out from the Zawichost all Jewish.
And they came then in [INAUDIBLE].
This is [INAUDIBLE], you know?
And after, a lot what they-- was a concentration camp.
And they find out to the same thing.
From, from my city, the most of my city, you know, they killed them.
They never was in concentration camp.
A lot, they run away before.
They took the concentration.
Now, a lot, what they lived there.
And they not run away, none.
They took them.
And they killed them.
They shot them out there.
This I know of sure.
And, and after, you know, I would go see there, you know?
[INAUDIBLE], a lot they told me.
It was so many years, there was, you know, in 1946, there was September '39.
A lot they went there and they saw the, the, the graves, the all graves there.
So I, I, I couldn't go there.
Well, I was a-- the teacher told me, go away, you know?
And I never went there.
It's not far from Zawichost, 8, 8 kilometers.
I will see the graves there where they do-- in this-- in this-- when they come in the city for-- and [INAUDIBLE] You know, [INAUDIBLE], where the buried the old Jewish people, you know?
There is a road, Ozarow, Ozarow [INAUDIBLE].
They took from all the stones.
They took the stones away, and they make a road, a road to, to Ozarow, you know?
This was the [INAUDIBLE].
They took all the-- all the-- all the graves and stones.
This I won't see.
But they told me this.
This, I never was there.
If I couldn't go there, you know?
I was afraid this.
Did you return to the house that you lived in, in Zawichost--
No.
No, no.
No.
[INAUDIBLE].
Yeah, the--
Yeah, the Polish--
The Polish was, of course, with the Germans.
This is the reason why Poland, the concentration camp I, I lived was in Poland.
But the Polacks, they killed them, and they took away-- they took away the goods from the-- from the old Jewish products, all.
It-- Huh?
No.
I didn't.
A day.
A day, I was sleeping with him.
He gives me money.
He eat.
And he told me go away.
Where I went, I went to Austria.
Yeah, in Paris, Austria, this was Salzburg.
And there--
There I got from, from UNRRA, UNRRA, you know, Jewish Committee, you know, a passport, you know?
I can travel all over, you know, and look for my family. you understand, that was.
You know, in Salzburg [INAUDIBLE].
And, and they give me a-- and I was traveling.
I was in Paris [INAUDIBLE].
I find there two cousins.
Austria?
Austria, I was about a week.
I find-- in Salzburg, I find a cousin, [INAUDIBLE].
She was in concentration camp, you know?
And I find her.
How I find, well, there was the Jewish Committee.
There was names, a lot of names this.
I looked at names.
I see [INAUDIBLE] Guzman, you know?
Oh, but-- And I went there.
And I find out.
And, and I was finding her there.
She, she weighed 80 pounds.
She was, you know-- She was alive.
After in Paris, I went there, you know?
I find-- I find two cousins in Paris.
One cousin got killed in the French army, and one cousin died in 1977.
Why I went to Paris?
To look some of the girl what I know before the war.
They went away to Paris, you know?
[INAUDIBLE].
Let me see.
And I got transportation free, you know?
I was looking all the way, you know?
And I find-- I find a lot-- a lot in Zawichost [INAUDIBLE].
But they-- but they run away.
And they was in Austria, in Lviv, Lviv, Lemberg.
They was-- you know, I find when I was coming back, you know?
And From Paris, where did you go?
From Paris-- from Paris I was there, and I went to Italian from Paris, you know?
[BACKROUND NOISE]
I went-- Why, you see, my cousin in America, you know, he told me I will go to Israel.
I was written to go to Israel, you know?
Though my cousins, you know, they told me, you live so much, you know.
You going to this.
We take you to-- to America.
We take you to America.
This is the reason I stop to Israel, you know?
Though there was special lagers.
Who wants to go to America?
Who wants to go any place-- transport.
Before you go away, you know, they try.
This was when-- UNRRA, UNRRA, this is a, a committee, you know?
[INAUDIBLE] goyim, you know, all together, you understand?
United Nations Committees.
Yeah, United Nations [INAUDIBLE].
I was waiting there, you know, in, in Grugliasco, Turino Grugliasco.
There was camps.
I have pictures of that.
In-- I was there.
I was-- to America.
Now, after I got family in Brazilia, they send me papers, you know, to go to Brazilia.
My, my [NON-ENGLISH], kinder, yeah, yeah, sister's-- there's a cousins.
And no, I not want there.
My cousin is in America.
You come to America.
That's not.
That was waiting to [INAUDIBLE] Italian, though they told me they need-- they need-- you are supposed to wait.
I got a quota, a quota.
To wait-- make for a quota to go to America.
Takes a few years.
You understand?
The-- I tell you why I wait so long, though it's coming special [INAUDIBLE].
A special [INAUDIBLE] they take to Canada.
Either I think that they take me to Canada.
And from Canada to America, it's not far.
So there was a Jewish ORT, ORT class-- ORT children.
In Italia.
So I learn.
They need four years, special [INAUDIBLE] four years, you know?
I'm a tinsmith, you know?
In there, [LAUGHS] you know?
I tell you, if this, I go to learn this.
That was the Jewish Committee, you know, the ORT?
And they took me, and I learned there.
In two months, I got-- I got a permit, you know?
I'm a furrier.
[LAUGHS] Operator.
In, in not long, you know, took some few weeks, and I was coming to Canada.
Where did you-- where did you meet your first wife?
In, in Italia.
In Italia, I got married there.
She's, she's from Romania.
She's a Romanian Jewish.
Huh?
Survivor, yeah.
They went to Israel.
She was in Israel with the-- with the mother and her brother.
Then when I got married in [INAUDIBLE], you understand, she told me, you know, she has a brother with her mother, with this, And you saw they were coming to Canada, you know?
Now, we make up, you know, papers with all us, you know.
And they come from Israel to Canada.
And we got the house, you know, apartment, nice apartment in Canada.
And, and they're living with us here in this.
And they lived with us, and it was slow.
I was living there four, four, four years, five years, with there.
We was got a good marriage.
And since I took them, you know, there was trouble.
Why?
But she don't like me.
She's a Romanian.
She's Jewish.
She tells me, you got married with a Polish [INAUDIBLE], you know?
[LAUGHS] I don't know if you understand.
This is a Jewish, you know?
But you know, got married with a, a Romanian, not, not a Polish Jew-- [LAUGHS] Polish Jew.
But the mother-in-law, she was such a-- I tell you.
And we got argument.
And I told, you won't come to the United States to my wife.
And she, she tole me, OK, you go first.
I took my cousin.
My cousin went to Canada, you know?
And [INAUDIBLE].
He told me, if she not want.
Now, I was talking with her.
Everything she tell me, OK.
Now, I was coming to Canada, to United States, you know?
I, I got a job in a-- from my cousin.
He is David Rosen.
He has his baker's supply, big, big company, you understand?
He wants me make a baker-- sales baker.
And I was working with him, you understand that.
So when did you come to the United States?
The United States?
When I got divorced.
In Canada.
I got divorced.
This was in '57.
Then I came to United States.
And she told me, you go.
You make something.
And I'm coming.
And after I have friends in Canada, they told me, you know, with the best friends, I went then.
They, they took my wife away.
[LAUGHS] And, and she, she not want to come.
And after, you know, I got the-- in England, I remember, my aunt, Mama's sisters, and they send me-- they send me a diamond ring.
I, I-- they send me a diamond when I got married from Canada.
She, she sent me that parcel.
In the parcel she told me, she has in there a ring, you know, a diamond ring with money [INAUDIBLE] and she says, a pant.
There was a pant, you know, [INAUDIBLE] pant.
And, and I got this package.
And there was [LAUGHS] a [INAUDIBLE].
Yeah, and they send me silver candles from England.
You know, [INAUDIBLE], you know, in-- and I got this from my aunt.
She gives me them back.
And I told my wife there, next wife, give me the ring.
This said, this, no.
She not give me it.
So she got married with a goldsmith.
He makes a gold ring, you know?
He took off-- he took off the diamond.
He-- [LAUGHS] she send the empty ring.
So where did you go in the United States?
United States, my-- right away, you know?
My cousin, yeah, to New York, yeah.
To my family.
They took me a-- they took me apartment, my cousin here, you know?
He wants I should live with him, you know?
I was-- I was live with him two weeks, you know?
After he took me, you know.
And I was living.
And what kind of occupation did you do once you came to the United States?
I told you.
I was working for him [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] here.
You know, before I was working no furrier.
I was come in October, I remember.
Till December, I was working furrier.
When-- in Canada I was working, I make $80, $90 a week furrier. $90 a week.
I was coming.
He-- I never took [INAUDIBLE] I never ask for a price.
They give me $180.
I say, oh, the America, it's good, you understand?
There, you know, I make $80, $90 and the rent costs [INAUDIBLE].
It was good, you know?
After-- till December and all of a sudden, no work.
No work.
So my cousin took me [INAUDIBLE].
You understand?
I went to my cousin, you know?
I used to make all the [INAUDIBLE], you know?
My employment I couldn't get.
I must have 20 weeks, they told me.
So my cousin took me into [INAUDIBLE].
And after, I see there is fights.
My cousin with the wife, they fight.
He pays me.
If I'm not in money, I not do nothing.
You understand this?
I told him, you know, Dave, I don't like this job.
So I find out my [INAUDIBLE] man, he knows my father, my mother, my grandfather.
He's a furman by [INAUDIBLE] shoes and bags.
Now, I called him up.
[INAUDIBLE] you know, I said, [INAUDIBLE], my-- who you?
I'm your [INAUDIBLE].
Oh, [INAUDIBLE] Come Monday in the morning, 801 Broadway.
And I tell you if, if I give you the job.
I tell you, I don't know you.
How you look?
He told me, I'm a small guy.
[LAUGHS] You see me 10 to 8:00. 10 to 8:00 I be there in Broadway, go to work.
I went there.
I saw.
I paid.
I got up 6 o'clock in the morning.
I see him [INAUDIBLE] eventually, you know?
He goes there.
And I ask him, you, you [INAUDIBLE]?
He tell you.
He tell me, Samuel, you [INAUDIBLE]?
Yeah.
I went up.
He told me, you know, but the shoes is no work so much.
But it's Christmastime, this.
He told me, but the pocketbooks.
I tell you, yeah, what's the difference, you know?
And I start-- he is a-- the furman was by the pocketbooks, you know?
And he give me the job.
Over time, I ask for that price, you know?
I used to make $180.
I used to work for $52.
I started at $52 a week.
And I was working there, you know, believe me.
And I see who makes money there, pieceworker, cutters for the cut and framers.
They made the money.
I went there, you know, I went there.
In about six months, I tell you, I, I want be a framer.
And they raise me, and I start framing, you know?
I used to make, you know-- I never make so much money.
I used to make over $300.
I used to make tickets, you know?
I not give every-- I could make more.
But the boss, when he sees the tickets, I make so much money, you understand?
He cut the prices.
I give every week, you know, I make the price $290, $300.
And I was working 35 years of this job.
Why?
Even they want business.
I not want the business.
And this was [INAUDIBLE].
And I like it that this was mine did.
Let me ask you, how did you meet your second wife?
The second wife, I went out, you know.
And I went out a lot.
And I, I went out a lot.
[LAUGHS] Yeah.
Now, she-- I went out-- I, I went in the country with my [INAUDIBLE] cousin, [?
Beatrice. ?] And I see a girl sits separate, you know?
I turned to my cousin.
You know, this is a lonesome girl, you know?
I'll go see.
Now, I started talking with the-- with this.
And, and I like her very much, you know?
She's a-- the mother got six children, no father, you know?
But [INAUDIBLE] show me, you know, the mother was in Austria, oh, education, dull.
She used to play piano.
And Yiddish, she used to speak Yiddish, you know?
I used to love it, like.
And this is the reason I, I got-- I was love the motherland.
I got married with the daughter.
Were you--
This--
Yeah, yeah.
This is-- there was a, a familiar, you know?
It's not a rich family, poor, you know?
You know?
But her husband died.
He was about 40 years old.
He left six children.
You can imagine, you know?
And everybody was working.
I liked the family very much.
This is-- and she was such a good wife, I think.
She was so good.
And then passed away.
And I got a--
And I got a daughter with her, you know?
From the other, I couldn't have children with the-- with the first wife, no.
No, she-- ah, she was the best, best of mine.
And I got-- and I have now a daughter, you know, with two grandchildren.
I tell you.
This is all my life, I, I, I live just for them.
For not the children, I be not alive.
I got two heart attack, a bypass.
From them, from everything, now, you know, you get older.
Everything-- you know?
Yeah.
Let me tell you.
I was coming from the background of a very Orthodox family.
Now, it's hard to believe everything now.
Now, I'm not religious.
It's hard.
It's very hard for me, what I saw with my eyes, you know?
Pregnant woman where they cut, and this.
And, and what's happened this, you know, It's very hard for me at this-- to believe in this.
In God, I believe.
No, not like this.
And I change.
I change all my religion.
Yeah, this I go, for sure.
This, this, yeah.
You see?
Even-- every year.
I believe.
This-- not like this.
I never look of, of a [INAUDIBLE], you know?
Now, I know.
[INAUDIBLE] maybe you know, you know?
So many years, but over 57 years, you know?
I, I daven very good, very--
I remember when I was, you know, [LAUGHS] you don't forget this.
I used to speak Italian, Russian, Polish.
Well, too, Italian very good, you know?
Yiddish, never I forget.
Why?
For Yiddish, I speak every-- even my, my grandson, you know?
I speak Zeyde.
He come.
He says, Zeyde, Zeyde.
I speak Yiddish.
My daughter, I used to speak Yiddish always.
She understands everything.
Now, she answering in English here.
Oh.
I talk to her, but she was-- she's good.
And I have a son-in-law.
It's like it's true.
It's like a son.
In the beginning, I was a-- in the beginning, I-- I was against.
She shouldn't get married with him by.
But the family, it's not so for him, a lot-- a lot goyim.
You go.
You come together.
You know this?
I couldn't stand him.
It was like-- so I was against my daughter [INAUDIBLE].
No.
But she told me, what do you want?
I should [INAUDIBLE]?
I come to Boston, you know?
And she went to Boston College about three years.
She went there and met somebody, you know?
And she find out he's-- she found out he's a Christian.
But she knows.
And-- So what kind of-- what kind of Jewish education did your daughter have growing up?
She got-- I, I [INAUDIBLE] she learned in the Hebrew school, yeah.
I sent her.
In the Bronx.
I used to live there.
In the Bronx, in Jerome Avenue, there was a Jewish-- oh, she used to, till turned to me and she got-- she has a [INAUDIBLE].
She got bat mitzvah with everything, yeah.
Yeah.
I gave her.
Even the school she was in Boston, she went to Sunnybrook.
And after Sunnybrook-- and after she went to Boston, she took candles-- she Hanukkah lights.
You know Hanukkah?
She lit the candles in Friday.
Now, the husband, he never was [? relig ?] You know, the mother, you know, I tell you, the whole family was goyim, the most.
Now, my daughter, you know, are Jewish home, kosher, kosher, [INAUDIBLE].
Even I'm not [INAUDIBLE] very kosher.
And, and they want the children [INAUDIBLE] this is.
And, and I'm, I'm not against.
Do what you want.
It's your home.
Now, That have a house.
They live in Oceanside.
They bought two years ago.
And every day, you should see my grandson, four years old.
Zeyde, how you-- how you feeling?
[LAUGHS] Just let me ask you one other question.
What, what effect had your experiences had on the way you brought up your daughter?
You brought her up a certain way because--
Yeah, I--
I-- oh, she know.
I brought her up.
She know my family, and my [INAUDIBLE].
This is the reason.
She never-- she never mix, mix this.
I tell you, if I have you, if you get married with, with something Christian, you know, even I have-- I don't want-- I want you-- I want-- no, I say, [INAUDIBLE] this.
I brought her up very, very-- not Orthodox, you understand?
There is a-- now, you say conservative, you know?
Yeah.
Very, very-- and now, she's, she's so Orthodox.
I, I, I mean it.
She's a good kid.
Is there anything you'd like to add, any question that you feel that I've not asked you?
You know, I feel, you see-- this from Poland.
When I remembered from Poland when, when they told me the Polacks, they save a lot of Jewish Jews.
I know-- I know in mine, this, since I was a small, you know, till seventh year is the blood always was problem.
Well, it's not my city.
But my city, they know there is a-- there is a [INAUDIBLE], you know?
They can beat them [INAUDIBLE] up.
You know, in other cities, even [INAUDIBLE] was a bigger city, Ozarow always by.
But the Jewish, they got stores, you understand?
Then the Polack, they won't come in.
They never pay.
This is, is [INAUDIBLE].
Swindlers, they told the Jewish.
And always was fights, always was problem, always.
Every week was markets, you know, markets, you know?
This, this on there, you know?
But I, I can never forget.
And this is the reason my daughter, she knows this, you know, what the-- what the Christian do.
They go Sunday in the church.
After, after they pray, they go drinking in the bars, you know?
Who has the bars?
The Jewish.
They have the bars.
They drinking.
And after, yeah, after drunk, and this-- [INAUDIBLE] you steal.
You swindle.
You this.
And this is this.
This was Poland all my years since I went to school, since this, always.
This is the reason [INAUDIBLE] was working here.
My, my uncle, he was a tinsmither, a blacker, you know?
He was working with goyim.
He got-- he got jobs from them.
But they was rich, you know, Christian, you know?
They know he was the best-- the best roofer, you know, the best roofer in Zawichost, the shtetl.
And they thought [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE], all countries what they was [INAUDIBLE].
He was a prince, like prince, rich, very rich.
They have a lot of places.
Now, after this, they, they finished their two months, three months working.
I remember though they give us the prince flower, potatoes, carrots, cabbage, for all winter, you understand?
I remember.
But he was very good to us.
Do you have any desire to visit Poland today?
No, no, never.
I couldn't go.
My cousin-- my cousin went there, want to take me, you know, to Zawichost.
She went there.
I, I not-- I not have nothing, nothing.
What's the interest?
They took away everything, you know?
And you see, when the [INAUDIBLE] is here, I know, you know?
I go [INAUDIBLE] with my grandfather, you know, my, my [INAUDIBLE], my this.
You can.
Yeah, this is-- there is.
Maybe, I don't know.
He asked me, [INAUDIBLE], last week.
I, I was there.
There was a bat mitzvah in Pennsylvania, you know?
I was with my family, with the cousins.
I have cousins, his second cousins, you know?
They tell me, Samuel, you want go-- you want go to Poland?
I said where?
They go chase the whole-- they go around, around, you know.
I tell you, no, I don't feel so good.
I, I couldn't-- I couldn't go.
And it's true.
I got a car, you know?
I can drive it.
And I give away the car, you know?
Now, my arthritis and this.
And I not feel so good.
But medicine, [INAUDIBLE].
I just was by the doctor.
I was coming in about 12:30.
I have appointment.
I can sleep at night.
Thank you very much, Mr.
Bekler.
That's it.
